name: Ruthenium
name_origin: From 'Ruthenia', the Latin name for Rus' (a region encompassing parts of modern Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus), in honor of the homeland of its discoverer Karl Ernst Claus.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Ruthenium is one of the rarest metals in Earth's crust at about 0.001 ppm. It is found in platinum group metal (PGM) ores in South Africa, Russia, and Canada, and is recovered as a byproduct of platinum and nickel refining.

compounds.name: Ruthenium tetroxide
compounds.summary: A volatile, toxic, and powerful oxidizer used as a stain in electron microscopy and as an oxidant in organic synthesis.

compounds.name: Ruthenium dioxide
compounds.summary: An electrically conductive oxide used in resistors, capacitors, and as an electrocatalyst (e.g., in chlor-alkali cells).

compounds.name: Ruthenium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: A common precursor in ruthenium organometallic chemistry and homogeneous catalysis.

compounds.name: Tris(bipyridine)ruthenium(II) chloride
compounds.summary: A photoactive complex widely used in dye-sensitized solar cells and photoredox catalysis.

compounds.name: Ruthenium-carbene catalysts
compounds.summary: A family of ruthenium alkylidene complexes that revolutionized olefin metathesis (Nobel Prize 2005).

uses: Hardening alloying agent for platinum and palladium in jewelry and electrical contacts
Catalyst in olefin metathesis (Grubbs catalysts) and ammonia synthesis
Electrodes for chlor-alkali cells (DSA - dimensionally stable anodes)
Thick-film resistors and chip resistors in electronics (RuO2)
Solar cell sensitizers in dye-sensitized photovoltaics
reactivity_summary: Ruthenium is a hard, lustrous platinum-group metal with excellent corrosion resistance. It does not tarnish in air at room temperature, is unaffected by most acids including aqua regia, and dissolves only in molten alkalis or oxidizing fusion mixtures. It readily oxidizes at high temperatures to form RuO4, which is volatile and highly toxic. Ruthenium exhibits a remarkable range of oxidation states from -2 to +8.
isotopes_summary: Ruthenium has seven naturally occurring isotopes: 96Ru (~5.54%), 98Ru (~1.87%), 99Ru (~12.76%), 100Ru (~12.60%), 101Ru (~17.06%), 102Ru (~31.55%), and 104Ru (~18.62%). Several radioisotopes are produced in nuclear reactors as fission products; 106Ru (half-life 374 days) is used in brachytherapy for ocular tumors.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
